metadata.title: The Sponsorship Tier That Flips: Why the Famous Brand Is the Wrong Target
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of The Reallocation Engine card 02. Source chapter: the-reallocation-engine/chapters/07-who-sponsors-the-80-days-sponsorship-scorer.md. The mechanism: a composite sponsorship scorer built from three public datasets produces a ranking flip — the famous brand scores Avoid while the unknown biotech scores Proven.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = Proven / filing history / the unknown biotech (the good target); CRIMSON = Avoid / prestige / no filings (the wrong target); GOLD = editor's-pen highlight, once per graphic; never swapped mid-film
metadata.note: EXCLUSIONS honored: NO deep dive into LCA filing mechanics or USCIS process steps; NO discussion of the name-matching/entity-resolution problem (that is card 03); NO immigration-law advice; NO equations shown as math notation — the weighted formula is narrated and visualized as a stacked bar, never printed as a formula. The Unknown-vs-Avoid distinction appears only as a passing reference; it is card 03's territory.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B01: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B14: palette=claude but the outro is 'OutroCTA' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Marcus has two targets. One is a household name he has worn on a t-shirt. The other is a fourteen-person biotech no one has heard of.

card.copy: Why the Famous Brand Is the Wrong Target

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [TITLE] The sponsorship tier that flips

beats.narration_text: He puts the brand at the top of his list. He puts the biotech at the bottom. He applies to the brand first, waits three weeks — and hears nothing.

props.headline: He puts the brand at the top of his list. He…

beats.scene_description: a job candidate's handwritten target list pinned to aged newsprint: the famous company logo near the top with a star, a small unknown biotech name at the bottom barely legible — editorial collage style, desaturated, flat print
beats.image_prompt: B02, a handwritten job-search target list on lined notebook paper pinned to aged newsprint, top entry is a well-known company name with a hand-drawn star, the bottom entry is a small biotech name barely noticed, editorial collage treatment, desaturated flat newsprint scan, no people, close crop on the list
beats.new_visual_element: [STILL] Marcus's ranked target list — famous brand at top

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [STILL] Marcus's ranked target list — famous brand at top

beats.narration_text: Prestige should predict sponsorship. Here is the case where a famous company scores Avoid and an unknown one scores Proven. Why?

card.dek: Here is the case where the famous brand scores Avoid — and the unknown biotech scores Proven. Why?

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] THE QUESTION — gap formula on screen

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] THE QUESTION — gap formula on screen

beats.narration_text: A company can be famous without ever filing the paperwork to sponsor a visa. And it can be unknown and file thirty times. The name does not carry that information. The record does.

production_viz.label: name vs record — two separate signals
production_viz.mechanic: two columns appear: left column 'PRESTIGE' (the brand name chip in CRIMSON), right column 'FILING RECORD' (a stack of small teal bars); an X mark over an arrow connecting them — no reliable link
production_viz.note: establishes the gap: prestige and filing record are independent

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] name vs record — two separate signals

beats.narration_text: A company that has sponsored has built the legal infrastructure, engaged immigration counsel, and paid the fees. That is a decision. A reputation is not a decision.

production_viz.label: revealed behavior vs reputation
production_viz.mechanic: two cards side by side: TEAL card 'FILING RECORD — revealed behavior' with a SerifLabel annotation; CRIMSON card 'REPUTATION — not a decision'; the teal card grows slightly as it is named the load-bearing signal
production_viz.note: card contrast — the chapter's 'revealed behavior' language

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] revealed behavior card vs reputation card

beats.narration_text: The scorer combines four signals from three public records: the LCA filing rate at forty percent weight, H-1B approval rate at thirty, funding recency at twenty, and company size at ten.

production_viz.label: four weighted components
production_viz.mechanic: a single horizontal bar divided into four segments, left to right: LCA filing rate 40% (TEAL, widest), approval rate 30% (TEAL), funding recency 20% (SLATE), size 10% (SLATE); each segment labeled with a SerifLabel below; bar builds left to right
production_viz.note: the formula as a visual bar — no printed equation

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] weighted bar — four components build left to right

beats.narration_text: The LCA filing rate carries the most weight because filing is the most direct revealed action in the public record. Forty percent of the score hinges on whether a company has actually done this.

production_viz.label: LCA rate — the dominant signal
production_viz.mechanic: start from the full bar of B06; the LCA segment pulses teal, then a bracket + SerifLabel 'DOMINANT SIGNAL — 40%' appears below it; other segments fade slightly
production_viz.note: isolate the LCA weight as the critical point

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] LCA segment highlighted — dominant signal bracket

beats.narration_text: Now run the biotech. Fifteen LCAs over three years. Eighty-five percent approval rate. Series A eight weeks ago. These are illustrative numbers, but the shape is real: all four segments build. The composite lands in Proven.

production_viz.label: HelixBio — stacked bar builds to Proven
production_viz.mechanic: a stacked vertical bar labeled 'HelixBio' rises from the bottom: four teal/slate segments stack in order LCA, approval, recency, size; each segment appears with a SerifLabel showing its contribution; a TEAL 'PROVEN' chip lands at the top when complete
production_viz.note: illustrative numbers — labeled as such in FACTCHECK; manim move: compare (building bar)

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] HelixBio stacked bar — builds segment by segment to PROVEN

beats.narration_text: Now run the famous brand. LCA rate for roles like yours: essentially zero. That forty-percent term contributes nothing. No relevant approvals. The filing and approval segments — seventy percent of the score — are empty. The composite collapses to Avoid.

production_viz.label: SponsorCo — bar collapses to Avoid
production_viz.mechanic: a stacked vertical bar labeled 'SponsorCo' beside HelixBio's completed bar; LCA and approval segments appear as empty outlines (CRIMSON stroke, no fill); funding and size segments fill in SLATE; a CRIMSON 'AVOID' chip lands at the pathetically low bar; the two bars now sit side by side — the visual flip
production_viz.note: the ranking flip is visible: tall teal vs short crimson

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] SponsorCo bar — empty 70% collapses to AVOID beside PROVEN

beats.narration_text: The tier is what the public record supports. Four datasets, all free, all public. The record always outranks the reputation — and you did not know until you looked.

production_viz.label: four-tier spectrum — Avoid to Proven
production_viz.mechanic: a horizontal spectrum arrow from CRIMSON 'AVOID' on the left through SLATE 'UNKNOWN' and 'LIKELY' to TEAL 'PROVEN' on the right; small action cues below each tier as SerifLabels; HelixBio and SponsorCo chips drop to their positions
production_viz.note: still lanes: geo (horizontal spectrum from Avoid to Proven with action cues per tier)

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] four-tier spectrum with company chips landing on their tiers

beats.narration_text: Marcus's example, with illustrative numbers. SponsorCo: zero LCAs, zero approvals, composite roughly zero-point-zero-five. Skip. HelixBio: twelve LCAs, eighty-three percent approval, Series A eight weeks ago, composite roughly zero-point-eight-seven. Apply. His instinct ranked them opposite. The data did not.

production_viz.label: Marcus's two composites — the flip made concrete
production_viz.mechanic: two entity cards side by side: left CRIMSON-bordered 'SponsorCo' with MONO '0.05' and CRIMSON 'SKIP' chip; right TEAL-bordered 'HelixBio' with MONO '0.87' and TEAL 'APPLY' chip; below both: a SerifLabel 'Marcus ranked them opposite. The data did not.'
production_viz.note: illustrative numbers — labeled in FACTCHECK; the example act

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] Marcus's two score cards — SKIP vs APPLY

beats.narration_text: Prestige should predict sponsorship. But the company that has built the infrastructure and paid the fees has demonstrated something a reputation cannot. The record always outranks the name.

card.copy: The record always outranks the reputation.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] endcard — record outranks reputation

beats.name: OutroSeries
